# Introduction

LED driver systems are widely used in lighting, displays, automotive applications, and many embedded systems. In modern aircraft, LED lighting systems are used for cabin illumination, safety lighting, signage, and many other functions. These systems must be highly reliable, efficient, and suitable for the demanding conditions of aviation.

Mixed-signal LED drivers combine analog current-driving circuits with digital control logic. The digital part is responsible for controlling PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation), managing configuration registers, coordinating interfaces, and ensuring correct timing behaviour. Before a mixed-signal integrated circuit can be manufactured, its digital subsystem needs to be carefully designed, verified, and validated through FPGA prototyping and behavioural modelling.

This thesis focuses on the initial design of the digital part of a mixed-signal LED driver. The work includes requirements analysis, behavioural modelling, RTL design in SystemVerilog, hardware validation on a GateMate FPGA, and using IIC-OSIC-TOOLS suite, including cocotb,and documenting.


## Motivation and Challenges

The efficiency of a serial daisy-chain architecture for scalable and cuttable lighting systems has been proven by addressable LED driver devices like the *WS2812B*. These devices allow for minimal wiring and straightforward system integration because configuration data is sent through a single data stream, and each node extracts its configuration according to its position in the chain.

With independently adjustable channel currents and high supply voltages, the daisy-chain architecture is extended to multi-channel LED control by the AES LED driver IC concept. This leads to deterministic end-to-end update timing across lengthy serial LED chains, flicker-free dimming, and strict PWM resolution requirements.
